title: Whodunit?
text: Uh oh! original Palmer Penguins (penguins) data set badly misshapen. Variable names missing, categories categorical variables replaced letters, numerical variables scaled, columns reordered. job use functions datadist package determine done original penguins data set resulted catastrophe. First, ’ll want install package.

title: Whodunit?
text: Let’s first figure happened categorical variables data set. First, can use chardist generate distance matrix every pairwise combination categorical variables two data sets. result already pretty telling, lets use dscore look variable mappings just confirm things. dist column give us distance score two variables data set best match. Great! Now know variables . , recall categorical variables data set recoded letters. Luckily, chardist_XY can help us solve problem. can use variable pairs tell us categories original data set represented letter. chardist_XY designed work single variable comparisons, results returned combination chardist dscore. , interpret results entirety? Well, use chardist dscore told us Var4 penguins_whodunit actually species original penguins data set, example (forth mappings). , take closer look actual factors Var4 species, can find “” actually “Adelie”, “B” actually “Chinstrap”, “C” actually “Gentoo”. can look closely variable mappings way. Now ’ve got categorical variables covered, let’s move numerical ones.
code: peng_dist <- chardist(penguins,penguins_whodunit) #> Warning in dscore(charmat): Rows and columns not equal. Best matches found. #> Warning in dscore(charmat): Rows and columns not equal. Best matches found. #> Warning in dscore(charmat): Rows and columns not equal. Best matches found. #> Warning in dscore(charmat): Rows and columns not equal. Best matches found. peng_dist #>               Var4      Var5       Var8 #> species 0.00000000 0.1970998 0.09275284 #> island  0.09275284 0.1507234 0.00000000 #> sex     0.19709978 0.0000000 0.15072336 #> attr(,"dfa") #> [1] "penguins" #> attr(,"dfb") #> [1] "penguins_whodunit" dscore(peng_dist)$mappings #>   penguins penguins_whodunit dist #> 1  species              Var4    0 #> 2      sex              Var5    0 #> 3   island              Var8    0 # dscore() actually returns a list of items # use $ + tab to see what else it returns! chardist_XY(penguins$species,penguins_whodunit$Var4)$distmat$mappings #> use the stringdist package to find a string distance between the values #>         dfA dfB dist #> 1    Adelie   A    0 #> 2 Chinstrap   B    0 #> 3    Gentoo   C    0 chardist_XY(penguins$sex,penguins_whodunit$Var5)$distmat$mappings #> use the stringdist package to find a string distance between the values #>      dfA dfB dist #> 1 female   A    0 #> 2   male   B    0 chardist_XY(penguins$island,penguins_whodunit$Var8)$distmat$mappings #> use the stringdist package to find a string distance between the values #>         dfA dfB dist #> 1    Biscoe   A    0 #> 2     Dream   B    0 #> 3 Torgersen   C    0

title: Whodunit?
text: numerical variables, goal simply match variables. Let’s run numdist two data sets see happens. Huh. ’s weird- none distance values indicate numerical variables penguins_whodunit good matches variables original penguins data set. ’s going ? Looking numerical variables penguins_whodunit data set, can tell normalized. , use numdist look distance matrix numerical variables, want set scale option TRUE. ? numdist relies wasserstein_XY, function computes Wasserstein distance two variables. Since computation Wasserstein distance relies distance actual data values , scale variables data sets computing Wasserstein distance get scale. , distance calculation two variables accurate. Ah, looks much better. Though, decimals ’s super easy tell variables match just looking . applying dscore look mappings come handy. dscore determined mappings seen result lowest distance score numerical variables. Thus, can interpret results similarly categorical variables. , Var1 penguins_whodunit data set originally bill_len variable original data set, likewise can interpret variable mappings. ’s noted none scores best matched variables exactly zero, though close. scaling; variables penguins_whodunit scaled begin , used scale=T argument ran numdist, variables scaled . , scaling variable second time doesn’t change values much allows proper comparison, just enough change result non-zero scores see .

title: Whodunit?
text: concludes sleuthing! using combination datadist functions, now gathered information needed figure variables , subsequently, information need convert penguins_whodunit back original penguins data set. hopefully along way, ’ve learned little datadist functions use .

title: Package for detecting differences in datasets
text: object kgl_diamonds contains five data 320 data sets available kaggle searching platform diamonds (Feb 2026). first glance, dimensions datasets similar , identical. dimensions also similar diamonds dataset ggplot2: datasets identical diamonds data? look bit closer, though, find “vittoriogiatti” strong similarities diamonds data ggplot2, names variables coincide numeric values , even order. picture get calculate marginal distances pairs variables two datasets: see 0s diagonal, indicating pairs variables marginal distribution, .e. variables identical data. also see difference x y small, indicating high positive correlation two measurements, .e. diamonds highly symmetric. data error: y = 58.9 diamonds priced $1460 $1540  matrix can summarized score calculated minimum sum possible variable variable mappings: value 0 dscore indicates variables datasets identical marginal distributions. Datasets additional variable row numbers saved first column: , however, identical : interestingly, number observations kgl_diamonds data sets 4 5 different original. maybe submissions used script update data?   Alas, .
code: data("kgl_diamonds") kgl_diamonds |> purrr::map(.f = dim) #> $`shivam2503/diamonds` #> [1] 53940    11 #>  #> $`lovishbansal123/diamond-dataset` #> [1] 53940    11 #>  #> $`vittoriogiatti/diamondprices` #> [1] 53940    10 #>  #> $`nancyalaswad90/diamonds-prices` #> [1] 53943    11 #>  #> $`amirhosseinmirzaie/diamonds-price-dataset` #> [1] 50000    10 library(ggplot2) library(dplyr) #>  #> Attaching package: 'dplyr' #> The following objects are masked from 'package:stats': #>  #>     filter, lag #> The following objects are masked from 'package:base': #>  #>     intersect, setdiff, setequal, union dim(diamonds) #> [1] 53940    10 kgl_diamonds |> purrr::map(.f = function(x) identical(x,diamonds)) #> $`shivam2503/diamonds` #> [1] FALSE #>  #> $`lovishbansal123/diamond-dataset` #> [1] FALSE #>  #> $`vittoriogiatti/diamondprices` #> [1] FALSE #>  #> $`nancyalaswad90/diamonds-prices` #> [1] FALSE #>  #> $`amirhosseinmirzaie/diamonds-price-dataset` #> [1] FALSE summary(kgl_diamonds[[3]] - diamonds) #>      carat     cut           color         clarity            depth   #>  Min.   :0   Mode:logical   Mode:logical   Mode:logical   Min.   :0   #>  1st Qu.:0   NA's:53940     NA's:53940     NA's:53940     1st Qu.:0   #>  Median :0                                                Median :0   #>  Mean   :0                                                Mean   :0   #>  3rd Qu.:0                                                3rd Qu.:0   #>  Max.   :0                                                Max.   :0   #>      table       price         x           y           z     #>  Min.   :0   Min.   :0   Min.   :0   Min.   :0   Min.   :0   #>  1st Qu.:0   1st Qu.:0   1st Qu.:0   1st Qu.:0   1st Qu.:0   #>  Median :0   Median :0   Median :0   Median :0   Median :0   #>  Mean   :0   Mean   :0   Mean   :0   Mean   :0   Mean   :0   #>  3rd Qu.:0   3rd Qu.:0   3rd Qu.:0   3rd Qu.:0   3rd Qu.:0   #>  Max.   :0   Max.   :0   Max.   :0   Max.   :0   Max.   :0 wasserstein <- numdist(kgl_diamonds[[3]], diamonds) round(wasserstein, 2) #>       carat depth table price    x    y    z #> carat  0.00  0.77  0.60  0.21 0.46 0.08 0.09 #> depth  0.77  0.00  0.08  0.21 0.71 0.71 0.74 #> table  0.60  0.08  0.00  0.21 0.54 0.54 0.57 #> price  0.21  0.21  0.21  0.00 0.21 0.21 0.21 #> x      0.46  0.71  0.54  0.21 0.00 0.00 0.07 #> y      0.08  0.71  0.54  0.21 0.00 0.00 0.04 #> z      0.09  0.74  0.57  0.21 0.07 0.04 0.00 #> attr(,"dfa") #> [1] "kgl_diamonds[[3]]" #> attr(,"dfb") #> [1] "diamonds" diamonds |> filter(between(price, 1000, 3000)) |> ggplot(aes(x = price)) + geom_histogram(binwidth=10) dscore(wasserstein)$score #> NULL wasserstein_1 <- numdist(kgl_diamonds[[1]], diamonds) round(wasserstein_1, 2) #>       carat depth table price    x    y    z #> X      0.50  0.50  0.50  0.43 0.50 0.50 0.50 #> carat  0.00  0.77  0.60  0.21 0.46 0.08 0.09 #> depth  0.77  0.00  0.08  0.21 0.71 0.71 0.74 #> table  0.60  0.08  0.00  0.21 0.54 0.54 0.57 #> price  0.21  0.21  0.21  0.00 0.21 0.21 0.21 #> x      0.46  0.71  0.54  0.21 0.00 0.00 0.07 #> y      0.08  0.71  0.54  0.21 0.00 0.00 0.04 #> z      0.09  0.74  0.57  0.21 0.07 0.04 0.00 #> attr(,"dfa") #> [1] "kgl_diamonds[[1]]" #> attr(,"dfb") #> [1] "diamonds" wasserstein_12 <- numdist(kgl_diamonds[[1]], kgl_diamonds[[2]]) round(wasserstein_12, 2) #>          X carat depth table price    x    y    z #> X     0.00  0.50  0.50  0.50  0.43 0.50 0.50 0.50 #> carat 0.50  0.00  0.77  0.60  0.21 0.46 0.08 0.09 #> depth 0.50  0.77  0.00  0.08  0.21 0.71 0.71 0.74 #> table 0.50  0.60  0.08  0.00  0.21 0.54 0.54 0.57 #> price 0.43  0.21  0.21  0.21  0.00 0.21 0.21 0.21 #> x     0.50  0.46  0.71  0.54  0.21 0.00 0.00 0.07 #> y     0.50  0.08  0.71  0.54  0.21 0.00 0.00 0.04 #> z     0.50  0.09  0.74  0.57  0.21 0.07 0.04 0.00 #> attr(,"dfa") #> [1] "kgl_diamonds[[1]]" #> attr(,"dfb") #> [1] "kgl_diamonds[[2]]" wasserstein_4 <- numdist(kgl_diamonds[[4]], diamonds) wasserstein_5 <- numdist(kgl_diamonds[[5]], diamonds) kgl_diamonds[[4]] |> filter(between(price, 1400, 1600)) |> ggplot(aes(x = price)) + geom_histogram(binwidth=10) kgl_diamonds[[5]] |> filter(between(price, 1400, 1600)) |> ggplot(aes(x = price)) + geom_histogram(binwidth=10) kgl_diamonds[[5]] |> ggplot(aes(x = x, y = y)) + geom_point()

title: Package for detecting differences in datasets
text: Different situations: comparison numeric categorical - sure , later categorical categorical (lowish number categories): Idea categorical distances: check X equal Y (works number observations) use dummy encoding X Y run numscore
code: full_x <- kgl_diamonds[[5]]$cut |> as.factor() |> ecdf()  table_x <- kgl_diamonds[[5]]$cut |> table() |> ecdf() # gives different ecdfs because of different orderings   wassersteinXY(kgl_diamonds[[5]], diamonds) #> [1] NA   # option 0: count all non-matching strings (for some string distance) (needs the same number of observations) char_dist <- sum(kgl_diamonds[[1]]$cut != sample(diamonds$cut))/length(kgl_diamonds[[1]]$cut)  # option 1: use dummy encoding (0/1 data.frames) then use datadist on those data frames dfA <- data.frame(model.matrix(~cut-1, data = kgl_diamonds[[5]])) dfB <- data.frame(model.matrix(~cut-1, data = diamonds))  dfA$name <- "A" dfB$name <- "A"

Mappings map best matched categories two variables.

what: Calculate the Wasserstein distance between two random variables — wassersteinXY
title: Calculate the Wasserstein distance between two random variables — wassersteinXY
text: Wasserstein distance two numeric variables X Y defined distance distribution functions $F_X$ $F_Y$ measured p-norm.

title: Calculate the Wasserstein distance between two random variables — wassersteinXY
code: wassersteinXY(X, Y, scale = FALSE, p = 1, knots = max(c(length(X), length(Y))))

title: Calculate the Wasserstein distance between two random variables — wassersteinXY
text: X real-valued random variable Y real-valued random variable scale binary value indicating whether variables normalized p non-negative value identifying norm. $p$ = 1 Manhattan norm, $p=2$ Euclidean distance. knots non negative integer value specifying number evaluations within ranges X Y.

title: Calculate the Wasserstein distance between two random variables — wassersteinXY
text: single positive value. Larger values indicate less similarity.

title: Calculate the Wasserstein distance between two random variables — wassersteinXY
code: x <- runif(1000) y <- runif(500) wassersteinXY(x, y, knots=50) #> [1] 0.02308 wassersteinXY(x, y, scale=TRUE, knots=50) #> [1] 0.0103 # compare to results from transport::wasserstein1d(x, y)
